name: OpenHands
description: OpenHands (formerly OpenDevin) is an open-source autonomous AI software engineer that can modify code, execute terminal commands, browse the web, open pull requests, and collaborate with users on software development. It runs inside sandboxed Docker environments and supports any LLM via OpenRouter. 70K+ GitHub stars, 490+ contributors, MIT licensed.
oneLiner: Open-source autonomous AI software engineer that writes code, runs commands, and opens pull requests in sandboxed environments.
pricing: Open Source (MIT, free); OpenHands Cloud (hosted) with usage-based pricing
capabilities: Terminal command execution in sandboxed Docker
Model-agnostic (Claude, GPT-4, Gemini, Llama via OpenRouter)
File system navigation and code search

uxAnalysis.builderFlow: Chat-based interface where users describe tasks or point to GitHub issue URLs. Agent reads the issue, plans an approach, writes code, runs tests, and opens a PR. Planning Mode (beta) adds a structured approval step before execution. Self-hosted via Docker or managed via OpenHands Cloud.
strengths: 70K+ GitHub stars — one of the most popular AI coding agents
Top SWE-bench performer for autonomous issue resolution
Strong Docker sandboxing for safe code execution
Model-agnostic — works with any LLM via OpenRouter
MIT licensed with active academic and industry community
Natural GitHub workflow — issues in, PRs out
weaknesses: Single-agent, code-only — not a general enterprise agent platform
No enterprise system integrations (CRM, ERP, etc.)
No governance, compliance, or team features
Quality varies significantly by task complexity
No built-in monitoring or cost management
notableFeatures: Planning Mode beta — structured plan-then-execute with approval
End-to-end GitHub issue to PR workflow

recentDevelopments: 2026-03: v1.6.0 with Kubernetes support and Planning Mode beta
2026-01: OpenHands Index launched — broader evaluation beyond SWE-bench covering greenfield, frontend, testing
2025: 53%+ on SWE-bench Verified when paired with Claude 4.5

deploymentOptions.notes: Open-source (MIT). Self-host via Docker. Cloud platform also available.

subCriteria.name: Agent Builder UX
subCriteria.rationale: Chat-based interface. Point at GitHub issue URL and agent works on fix. Natural interaction.

subCriteria.name: Time-to-Value
subCriteria.rationale: Docker-based setup. OpenHands Cloud simplifies but self-hosted requires Docker knowledge.

subCriteria.name: Debugging & Testing
subCriteria.rationale: Custom instructions and model selection. Limited agent behavior customization beyond prompts.

subCriteria.name: Documentation & Learning
subCriteria.rationale: Good docs for setup and basic usage. Academic paper provides technical depth. Community growing.

subCriteria.name: Multi-Agent Coordination
subCriteria.rationale: Single-agent only. No multi-agent orchestration.

subCriteria.name: Human-in-the-Loop Design
subCriteria.rationale: Chat interaction and Planning Mode for approval. PR review as natural HITL checkpoint.

subCriteria.name: Workflow Templates
subCriteria.rationale: No team collaboration features. Individual developer tool.

subCriteria.name: Team Development
subCriteria.rationale: No team collaboration features. Individual developer tool.

subCriteria.name: Native Connectors
subCriteria.rationale: Deep GitHub integration — reads issues, creates branches, opens PRs. Core workflow.

subCriteria.name: API & Protocol Standards
subCriteria.rationale: Terminal, file system, web browser. Can install and use any CLI tool. No pre-built connectors.

subCriteria.name: Authentication & Access
subCriteria.rationale: Not designed for enterprise system integration. Code-repo focused.

subCriteria.name: Data Pipeline & RAG
subCriteria.rationale: Model-agnostic via OpenRouter. Any compatible LLM works.

subCriteria.name: Guardrails & Kill Switches
subCriteria.rationale: Docker isolation is excellent. All code runs in disposable containers.

subCriteria.name: Audit Trail & Compliance
subCriteria.rationale: No RBAC or team permissions. Single-user operation model.

subCriteria.name: RBAC & Access Control
subCriteria.rationale: No compliance certifications. OSS project, not enterprise SaaS.

subCriteria.name: Explainability & Transparency
subCriteria.rationale: Planning Mode provides approval gate. No content filtering or action limits.

subCriteria.name: Deployment Flexibility
subCriteria.rationale: Docker-based self-hosted. OpenHands Cloud for managed. K8s support added in v1.6.0.

subCriteria.name: Scaling & Monitoring
subCriteria.rationale: Single-agent execution model. No built-in horizontal scaling or concurrent agent management.

subCriteria.name: Cost Management
subCriteria.rationale: Basic session logs. No production monitoring dashboard or alerting.

subCriteria.name: Ecosystem Maturity
subCriteria.rationale: 70K+ GitHub stars, 490+ contributors, 188+ academic/industry contributors. Massive for an OSS agent.

scoringRationale: 57/100 overall. Strongest: Integration (14/20). Weakest: Orchestration (6/20). Breakdown: Creation (13/20), Orchestration (6/20), Integration (14/20), Governance (11/20), Operations (13/20).
targetUser: Developers wanting AI to resolve GitHub issues and write code

walkthrough.title: How OpenHands Works: AI Agent Builds an App While You Watch

steps.title: Local Model with Devstral
steps.description: Download and configure Devstral model for fully local OpenHands execution. LM Studio for model management.

works: Fully local inference — zero API costs, complete data privacy
LM Studio GUI makes model management accessible
GGUF format runs on consumer hardware (M-series Macs)
pmCritique.userPsychology: Local model support is OpenHands' privacy differentiator. Enterprise teams with IP concerns can run coding agents without any data leaving the building.

ctoCritique.verdict: Local inference addresses our data privacy requirements. But model quality vs cloud API quality trade-off needs benchmarking.
concerns: Local model quality may be lower than cloud APIs
enterpriseGaps: No model governance for local deployments

steps.title: Docker Launch Configuration
steps.description: Docker pull and run with environment variables for LLM connection. One command to start the full agent environment.

works: Docker ensures consistent environments — no works-on-my-machine issues
Single docker run command — copy-paste deployment
doesntWork: Multiple terminal commands create error opportunities
pmCritique.userPsychology: Docker launch is the filter — developers with Docker start in 2 minutes, everyone else faces a 30-minute setup.

ctoCritique.verdict: Docker deployment is standard for our team. But production deployment needs Kubernetes, not docker run.
enterpriseGaps: No secrets management beyond env vars

steps.title: Fully Local Web UI
steps.description: Browser-based workspace running entirely locally. Chat panel, VS Code, Terminal, Jupyter, App, Browser tabs — complete dev environment.

works: Complete development environment in the browser — IDE + terminal + browser + notebooks
Runs entirely locally — no cloud dependency for the workspace
Multiple tabs cover every development workflow
doesntWork: Browser-based UI has performance limitations vs native IDE
No session persistence across Docker restarts
pmCritique.userPsychology: The local web UI is the compromise between cloud-hosted agents and terminal-only tools. It gives visual feedback while keeping everything on the machine.

ctoCritique.verdict: Local web UI is good for individual developers. But team-level agent deployment needs a shared platform, not individual Docker containers.

pmVerdict.summary: OpenHands is the strongest open-source AI coding agent — fully local execution, browser-based workspace, and real-time code generation. The split-panel UI (chat + IDE) creates a pair-programming experience no other tool matches. Devstral local model support means zero API costs and full data privacy. But: Docker prerequisite, no mid-task steering, and quality varies with model choice.
strengths: Fully local execution — zero API costs, complete data privacy
Split-panel UI (chat + VS Code + Terminal + Browser) is best-in-class for coding agents
Docker packaging ensures consistent environments across machines
risks: Docker is a hard prerequisite — blocks non-developers
No mid-task steering — can't redirect the agent once it starts
Code quality varies significantly with model choice
53% SWE-bench is impressive but means 47% failure rate

ctoVerdict.summary: OpenHands is the coding agent to evaluate for developer productivity. Fully local execution addresses data privacy concerns. Docker packaging ensures reproducibility. But: no enterprise support SLA, community-only help, and the 53% SWE-bench pass rate means it fails on half of real-world tasks. Use for augmenting developers, not replacing them.
strengths: Fully local — no data leaves the machine
53% SWE-bench is current state-of-the-art for open-source
dealBreakers: No IDE plugin — separate web UI required

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Task input, agent execution, code generation all demonstrated across 10 steps
scoringAlignment.gap: No template or starter project system

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Single agent only — no multi-agent coordination
Docker, local LLM, web UI shown. No external integrations
Docker deployment, local model monitoring, resource tracking demonstrated
scoringAlignment.gap: No production deployment or scaling shown

youtubeReviews.title: This AI Agent Built an App While I Watched (OpenHands + Daytona Demo)
OpenHands + Devstral = A Fully Local Coding Agent

coreCompetence.bestAt: The most capable open-source AI software developer — autonomous coding agent that can navigate codebases, write code, run tests, and interact with terminals.
coreCompetence.targetUser: Development teams who want an AI pair programmer that can autonomously tackle coding tasks. Open-source contributors who want full control over their AI coding assistant.
coreCompetence.vsAlternatives: Wins against closed-source alternatives (Devin, Cursor) on transparency and self-hosting. Wins on community contribution model. Loses to commercial products on polish and reliability. Loses to Cursor on IDE integration.
